questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset right flank pain radiating to the groin. He is nauseated but has no fever. Urine dipstick shows microscopic haematuria. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Classic renal colic with radiation to groin and microscopic haematuria, without fever or other systemic features, strongly suggests an obstructing ureteric stone. Pyelonephritis typically presents with fever, dysuria and loin pain. Renal infarction would have haematuria but no colicky radiation. Pancreatitis and AAA do not cause haematuria.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman at 28 weeks' gestation presents with right flank pain that is colicky and radiates to the groin. She reports nausea but no fever. Urine dipstick shows trace blood. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Colicky flank pain radiating to the groin with haematuria is classic for a ureteric stone, even in pregnancy. Physiological hydronephrosis is usually asymptomatic or dull bilateral pain, not colicky. Acute pyelonephritis would have fever and bacteriuria. Placental abruption presents with vaginal bleeding and abdominal pain, not colicky flank pain. Preterm labour involves uterine contractions.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old diabetic woman presents with high fever, rigors, and severe right flank pain of 12 hours' duration. She has a history of recurrent urinary tract infections and a known right renal stone. Urine dip shows nitrites and leucocytes. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of fever, rigors, flank pain, and an obstructing stone on history points to an infected kidney proximal to a ureteric stone (obstructive pyelonephritis). Acute cystitis does not cause flank pain. Renal abscess would be more indolent. Emphysematous pyelonephritis typically has gas on imaging and occurs in diabetics, but absence of gas findings makes obstructive less specific. Papillary necrosis usually has sloughed papillae.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with persistent right flank pain after passing a small kidney stone 2 days ago. He has no fever. CT scan shows a fluid collection adjacent to the right kidney without signs of infection. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Passing a stone can cause sudden decompression of the collecting system, leading to a forniceal tear and urine extravasation, forming a urinoma. Perinephric abscess would be septated and have inflammatory signs. Ureteric stricture develops more chronically. Renal vein thrombosis would have haematuria and proteinuria with acute pain. Retroperitoneal fibrosis is insidious and not acute.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old known recurrent calcium oxalate stone former presents with dull right loin pain of 2 weeks' duration. He has no fever, but urine dipstick shows non-visible haematuria. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The history of recurrent stone formation, dull pain, and persistent haematuria makes a new ureteric calculus most likely. Renal cell carcinoma often presents with painless haematuria or a mass, but can mimic. Papillary necrosis usually occurs in diabetics or analgesic abusers with sloughed papillae. Medullary sponge kidney is a congenital condition causing recurrent stones but not acute pain. Hypercalciuria is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe right loin pain radiating to the groin, associated with nausea and visible haematuria. He has no fever. What is the most appropriate first-line imaging investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Non-contrast CT of the kidneys, ureters and bladder (CT KUB)
questions.explanation: Non-contrast CT KUB is the first-line imaging for suspected renal colic in the UK, with high sensitivity for detecting ureteric stones. KUB X-ray has poor sensitivity for small or radiolucent stones; ultrasound is less sensitive for ureteric stones; IVU is no longer first-line; MRU is not used acutely.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with two previous episodes of calcium oxalate kidney stones is now stone-free after spontaneous passage. She has no known metabolic disease. What is the single most appropriate investigation to identify the underlying metabolic abnormality?
options: Serum parathyroid hormone and calcium level
Dual-energy CT to assess stone composition
Stone analysis of a previously passed stone
24-hour urine collection for calcium, oxalate, urate, citrate and volume
questions.explanation: A 24-hour urine collection (metabolic screen) is recommended for recurrent stone formers to detect hypercalciuria, hyperoxaluria, hyperuricosuria, hypocitraturia and low urine volume. Serum PTH is indicated if hypercalcaemia is found, but not as the first investigation. Stone analysis is complementary but does not assess all risk factors.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known left renal stone presents with fever (38.9°C), rigors and left loin pain. Urine dipstick is positive for nitrites and leucocytes. He is tachycardic and hypotensive. What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis of an obstructed infected stone?
options: Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis
questions.explanation: In suspected infected obstructed stone, non-contrast CT KUB is the imaging of choice to confirm the stone, hydronephrosis and perinephric stranding. It avoids contrast, which is relatively contraindicated in obstruction and sepsis. Ultrasound can show hydronephrosis but may miss a small obstructing stone. KUB and IVU are inadequate in this setting.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old pregnant woman at 20 weeks gestation presents with right loin pain and microscopic haematuria. She has no fever. She has a history of nephrolithiasis. What is the most appropriate imaging investigation to confirm the diagnosis of a ureteric stone?
questions.explanation: Ultrasound is first-line in pregnancy to avoid ionising radiation. It can identify hydronephrosis and most renal stones, though it is less sensitive for ureteric stones. If ultrasound is inconclusive, low-dose CT or MRU may be considered, but initial investigation is ultrasound. KUB and IVU involve radiation; MRU is reserved for second-line.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with a history of recurrent calcium oxalate stones is attending for routine follow-up after successful conservative management. He is currently asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate imaging to assess for new stone formation and monitor stone burden?
questions.explanation: Ultrasound is recommended for surveillance in recurrent stone formers to minimise radiation exposure. It can detect stones >3 mm and hydronephrosis. CT KUB is more sensitive but involves radiation and is reserved for acute presentations. KUB X-ray is less sensitive and not preferred for long-term follow-up.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with left flank pain. CT shows a 6 mm stone in the distal ureter, no hydronephrosis, and no signs of infection. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
questions.explanation: For a distal ureteric stone <10 mm without infection or obstruction, NICE recommends medical expulsive therapy with an alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) as first-line. Invasive interventions are reserved for failed conservative management or larger stones.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman has had three calcium oxalate stones over 2 years. 24-hour urine shows hypercalciuria (8 mmol/day) with normal serum calcium and eGFR >60 mL/min. What is the most appropriate prophylactic treatment?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends a thiazide diuretic (e.g., bendroflumethiazide) for recurrent calcium stones with hypercalciuria. Potassium citrate is for hypocitraturia, allopurinol for hyperuricosuria, and high fluid intake is baseline but insufficient alone.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a 12 mm proximal ureteric stone has ongoing pain despite analgesia and has failed conservative management. There is mild hydronephrosis but no infection. What is the most appropriate definitive stone treatment?
questions.explanation: For proximal ureteric stones >10 mm, URS has a higher stone-free rate than ESWL and is recommended as first-line definitive treatment. PCNL is for large renal stones, and MET is ineffective for stones of this size.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with recurrent UTIs and a staghorn calculus presents with fever, hypotension, and flank pain. CT shows an obstructing pelvic stone with hydronephrosis. Urinalysis is positive for nitrites and leukocytes. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Intravenous antibiotics and percutaneous nephrostomy drainage
Intravenous antibiotics and urgent ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy
Intravenous antibiotics and open stone removal
Intravenous antibiotics and extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy
questions.explanation: In septic obstructive uropathy from an infected stone, urgent decompression (nephrostomy or ureteric stent) is life-saving. Definitive stone treatment is delayed until sepsis resolves. Antibiotics alone are insufficient.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman at 28 weeks gestation presents with right flank pain and microscopic haematuria. Ultrasound shows an 8 mm stone at the vesicoureteric junction with mild hydronephrosis. She is afebrile. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Conservative management with analgesia and hydration
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, first-line management for a small, non-obstructing (or mildly obstructing) stone is conservative. ESWL is contraindicated in pregnancy. Invasive procedures (URS, stent, nephrostomy) are reserved for severe obstruction, infection, or refractory pain.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with sudden onset of severe right flank pain radiating to the groin. He is writhing in pain but has no fever, nausea, or vomiting. Urine dipstick shows blood 2+. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange an urgent ultrasound of the renal tract
Start intravenous fluids with normal saline
Perform a non-contrast CT of the kidneys, ureters and bladder (CT KUB)
questions.explanation: According to NICE guidelines (CG 118), the immediate priority in suspected renal colic is analgesia; an NSAID (e.g., diclofenac) is first-line because it is more effective than opioids for colic pain. CT KUB is indicated but not before pain relief. Ultrasound is less sensitive for small stones and is not the first step in an acute presentation.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man returns to the Urology Assessment Unit after being seen yesterday with a 5 mm distal ureteric stone confirmed on CT KUB. He has mild left hydronephrosis, is afebrile, and his serum creatinine is normal. His pain is controlled with oral analgesia. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Discharge with advice to increase oral fluid intake alone
Arrange an urgent MRI of the renal tract
Refer for emergency ureteroscopic laser lithotripsy
Start tamsulosin 0.4 mg once daily for medical expulsive therapy
questions.explanation: NICE recommends medical expulsive therapy with an alpha-blocker (e.g., tamsulosin) for distal ureteric stones >5 mm to increase the chance of spontaneous passage. There is no indication for MRI or urgent intervention as the patient is stable and not obstructed. Discharge without MET would miss an evidence-based treatment.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with a known 8 mm left proximal ureteric stone presents to the Emergency Department with a 2-day history of fever, rigors, and left flank pain. On examination, she is hypotensive (BP 85/50 mmHg), tachycardic (HR 115 bpm), and febrile (39.2°C). Urine dip shows leucocytes and nitrites. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start intravenous fluids and broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour
Insert a left ureteric stent or nephrostomy tube immediately
Take blood cultures and then await results
questions.explanation: This is obstructive pyelonephritis with septic shock. The immediate priority is resuscitation with IV fluids and early broad-spectrum antibiotics (within 1 hour) as per the Sepsis Six. Decompression (stent/nephrostomy) is essential but must follow initial stabilisation. CT scanning is not needed before decompression if the stone is already known. Inotropes are second-line after fluids.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man underwent extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL) for a 2 cm renal pelvic stone 3 days ago. He now presents with severe right flank pain and macroscopic haematuria. CT KUB shows a 'steinstrasse' (column of stone fragments) in the proximal ureter with moderate hydronephrosis. His temperature is 37.2°C and blood pressure is normal. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform repeat ESWL on the steinstrasse
Insert a ureteric stent to bypass the obstruction
Start tamsulosin and observe for 24 hours
Request a MAG3 renogram to assess obstruction
Arrange urgent ureteroscopy and laser fragmentation
questions.explanation: Steinstrasse causing hydronephrosis requires urgent decompression to prevent renal impairment or infection. The most immediate and minimally invasive method is insertion of a ureteric stent. Repeat ESWL is not effective for an obstructed column. Ureteroscopy is definitive but takes more time and preparation; therefore stenting is the immediate next step.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 28 weeks gestation presents with intermittent right flank pain and nausea. She has no fever. Urine dipstick shows blood 1+ but no nitrites or leucocytes. Renal tract ultrasound shows a 4 mm stone at the right vesicoureteric junction with mild hydronephrosis. Serum creatinine is normal. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start conservative management with oral hydration and simple analgesia
Arrange a non-contrast CT KUB with low-dose protocol
Insert a ureteric stent under ultrasound guidance
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, the first-line management of uncomplicated renal colic (small stone, no infection, controlled pain) is conservative (hydration and paracetamol-based analgesia). Most stones pass spontaneously. CT is contraindicated due to radiation, and MRI is not immediately needed. Invasive interventions (stent/nephrostomy) are reserved for refractory pain, obstruction, or infection.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with acute right flank pain radiating to the groin, with vomiting. He is in obvious distress. Which single finding on assessment would mark this as a urological emergency?
options: Stone visible on plain film measuring 4 mm
questions.explanation: Fever with an obstructing stone suggests pyonephrosis (infected obstructed kidney), a urological emergency requiring urgent decompression. Hypertension is common in pain, isolated mild creatinine rise is not emergency, haematuria is typical but not red flag, and small stones often pass spontaneously.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and a known left staghorn calculus is brought to the emergency department confused and febrile. Her observations show heart rate 110 bpm, blood pressure 85/50 mmHg, and respiratory rate 22/min. Which single finding is most concerning for a life-threatening complication of nephrolithiasis?
options: Urine dipstick showing 3+ leucocytes and nitrites
Systolic blood pressure <90 mmHg unresponsive to fluids
questions.explanation: Hypotension refractory to fluids indicates septic shock from obstructive uropathy (e.g., pyonephrosis or urosepsis). Leukocytosis, hyperglycaemia, pyuria, and flank tenderness are all present in this scenario but the hypotension is the critical sign of end-organ hypoperfusion requiring urgent intervention.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with recurrent calcium oxalate stones presents to the emergency department reporting no urine output for 12 hours. He has bilateral flank discomfort. Serum potassium is 6.1 mmol/L, creatinine 450 μmol/L, and pH 7.25. Which single laboratory finding most clearly mandates immediate urological decompression?
questions.explanation: Hyperkalaemia >6.0 mmol/L in the setting of anuric AKI due to bilateral obstructing stones poses imminent risk of cardiac arrhythmia, necessitating emergency decompression (e.g., nephrostomy or stenting). While creatinine elevation and hydronephrosis confirm obstruction, the immediate life threat is hyperkalaemia.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with a single functioning right kidney (left kidney atrophic from childhood reflux) presents with right flank pain and oliguria. His baseline creatinine is 100 μmol/L; now it is 220 μmol/L. Which single urinalysis finding is most concerning for a urological emergency?
options: Pyuria with gram-negative rods on microscopy
questions.explanation: In a solitary kidney, obstruction quickly leads to anuria and AKI. Pyuria with bacteria indicates infection in the obstructed system (pyonephrosis), which is a urological emergency requiring urgent drainage. Other findings are non-specific or non-emergent.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old primigravida at 28 weeks gestation presents with left flank pain and nausea. She has no fever or vaginal bleeding. Urinalysis shows trace blood but no nitrites. Which single finding on further assessment would indicate a complicated obstruction requiring emergency drainage?
options: Stone visible on ultrasound measuring 5 mm in the renal pelvis
Mild hydronephrosis on the left (renal pelvis 12 mm)
questions.explanation: Fever with rigors in pregnancy suggests infected obstructed kidney (pyonephritis), a high-risk emergency due to risk of sepsis and preterm labour. Small stone and mild hydronephrosis are common in pregnancy; normal creatinine and fetal heart rate are reassuring.
